metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Amend AC1 to explicitly require BOTH pipelines verified: e.g. 'the 3.13 Linux jobs in test.yml (GitHub Actions) AND gerrit-verify.yaml (Gerrit Verified +1) run green before the classifier is added'.
findings.finding: AC1 requires both test.yml AND gerrit-verify.yaml to be updated, but the verification clause 'the 3.13 Linux jobs run green in CI' is singular and does not explicitly require the Gerrit pipeline's 3.13 job to also be verified green. An executor could update both files but only confirm the GitHub Actions (test.yml) run, leaving the Gerrit workflow unverified.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC1, replace 'the 3.13 Linux jobs run green in CI' with 'the 3.13 Linux jobs run green in both test.yml (GitHub Actions CI) and gerrit-verify.yaml (Gerrit Verified +1 signal)'.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the plan whether the `pip-audit` cell-selector in gerrit-verify.yaml (`matrix.python-version == '3.12'`) should be updated to `'3.13'` (to track 'primary/newest') or intentionally left at `'3.12'` (stable sentinel), and update the adjacent comment accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan does not address the hardcoded `matrix.python-version == '3.12'` guard on the pip-audit step in gerrit-verify.yaml (line 245). The plan frames 3.13 as the new 'primary/newest' interpreter and explicitly calls for updating the macOS comment to reflect 3.13, but leaves the pip-audit cell-selector unmentioned. After the change 3.12 still exists in the matrix so the step does not break, but the plan's own 'primary interpreter' framing creates an implementer ambiguity: should the guard track to 3.13 (the new newest) or intentionally stay at 3.12?
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What / files' section, add: 'In gerrit-verify.yaml, update the pip-audit `if:` guard from `matrix.python-version == '3.12'` to `matrix.python-version == '3.13'` (keeping it on the newest/primary cell) and update the adjacent comment from "4× duplicate" to "6×".' If the intent is instead to keep it pinned to a stable value irrespective of newest, say so explicitly and leave the guard at '3.12'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sub-bullet to AC #1 (or a standalone AC) verifying that the matrix comment in both workflow files reflects 'macOS runs 3.13' after the exclude change.
findings.finding: The body describes a deliverable — 'update the matrix comment to say macOS runs 3.13' — that is not covered by any Acceptance Criterion, leaving it unverifiable at sign-off.
findings.location: What/files section (bullet 3: 'update the matrix comment to say macOS runs 3.13') vs. all four ACs
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC #1 to read: '…; the matrix comment in both files is updated to state macOS runs 3.13 only.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate (and explicitly call out as in-scope or intentionally out-of-scope) the hardcoded `python-version: "3.12"` pins in test.yml's `lint` job (line 40) and standalone `pip-audit` job (line 104).
findings.finding: test.yml contains two non-matrix jobs hardcoded to python-version: '3.12' — the `lint` job (line 40) and the standalone `pip-audit` job (line 104) — that the plan never enumerates as in-scope, out-of-scope, or acknowledged.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the 'What / files' section: 'The `lint` and `pip-audit` jobs in test.yml are pinned independently to 3.12 and are intentionally left unchanged (they are interpreter-agnostic utility jobs); OR update them to 3.13 to match the primary interpreter.' This makes the choice explicit either way.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge (in-scope or out-of-scope) the hardcoded `python-version: "3.12"` in gerrit-verify.yaml's `require-ticket` job (line 77) which is not covered by the matrix change.
findings.finding: gerrit-verify.yaml contains a `require-ticket` job with a hardcoded `python-version: "3.12"` (line 77) outside the build-and-test matrix that the plan never mentions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out note (e.g., 'To revert: restore the original single `(macos-latest, "3.11")` exclude in both workflow files') so the rollback path is documented in the plan.
findings.finding: The removal of the macOS+3.12 CI job (achieved by adding the `(macos-latest, "3.12")` exclude) is acknowledged and justified but the plan provides no explicit back-out/rollback instruction, as required by T4 when an existing CI behavior is changed.
findings.location: What/files section — exclude block change; Local-interpreter note; no rollback section exists
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line 'Rollback' note under the What/files section: 'Back-out: restore the exclude list to `[(macos-latest, "3.11")]` in both workflow files, removing the `(macos-latest, "3.12")` entry.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's assertion about README content: README.md contains no Python version statement; either confirm this is intentional (no README edit needed) or add the missing version wording as a named edit target.
findings.finding: The plan claims 'README states "Python ≥ 3.11"' as the basis for AC #3, but inspection of README.md reveals no such text — there is no Python version statement anywhere in the file.
findings.location: Plan section 'README supported-version wording' and AC #3
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'README states "Python ≥ 3.11"' with 'README currently has no Python version statement.' Then decide: either leave it as-is (AC #3 becomes 'README has no conflicting version claims') or add a supported-version line to README.md as an explicit edit target.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before opening the PR, audit `pyproject.toml` dependency declarations (and their PyPI pages / changelogs) to confirm all direct dependencies have published Python 3.13–compatible releases or wheels, and document the result in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 3.13 compatibility but does not verify that the project's declared dependencies (in pyproject.toml) are themselves known-3.13-compatible before committing the matrix and classifier changes.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pre-flight step to the plan: run `pip install --dry-run` (or `pip install`) against the locked/declared deps under a real 3.13 interpreter (e.g. via `pyenv` or a Docker image) and note any install-time failures. Record the outcome — 'all deps installed cleanly on 3.13 as of <date>' — so reviewers and future contributors have an evidence trail rather than relying solely on the CI run.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
